segments.text: Ever wondered how to supercharge your data science workflow with AI agents directly in VS Code? Today, I'll walk you through installing the HyperVelocity Engineering extension and using its AI-native agents to load, explore, and model the Titanic dataset — without writing any code yourself.

Let's start by installing the HyperVelocity Engineering, or HVE, extension in VS Code. Click the Extensions icon on the left sidebar — it looks like four squares. In the search bar that pops up, type 'HyperVelocity Engineering'. When you see it in the list, click the blue Install button. That’s it — no need for any API keys or credentials. This extension is ready to go out of the box.

Now, let’s explore the agents HVE adds to VS Code. Click the Copilot Chat icon — it looks like a speech bubble in the sidebar. A chat panel will open. In the agent picker at the top, you’ll notice several agent options, including @task-researcher, @task-planner, and @task-implementer. These are your AI partners for end-to-end data science tasks. We’ll use them step by step to work with the Titanic data.

First, let’s research the Titanic dataset. In the Copilot Chat panel, select @task-researcher from the agent picker. Type, 'Explore titanic.csv and summarize the data'. Hit Enter. The agent will create a research markdown file. To see the output, look in the explorer — you’ll find a new '.copilot-tracking/research' folder with a markdown file inside. Open it to review the automated analysis.

Next, let's plan the actual analysis. In the Copilot Chat panel, switch to @task-planner. Type, 'Plan a Python script to load, explore, and predict survival on titanic.csv'. The planner agent will create a plan file in the '.copilot-tracking/plans' folder. Check the explorer for this new plan. Open it to read through the detailed steps the agent has devised for our data science workflow.

It's time to bring it all together! Select @task-implementer in the Copilot Chat agent picker. Prompt it: 'Implement the plan for titanic.csv analysis in Python.' The agent generates the complete script — no copy-pasting required. Check the '.copilot-tracking/details' folder for the implementation details file. Open the file to see the ready-to-run Python code. Double-click the script to open it in the editor, then click the 'Run Python File' button at the top right of the editor. The terminal will display the output — likely some basic EDA and survival predictions using the Titanic data. This all happens automatically thanks to HVE's agent workflow.

We installed HVE Core, explored its agents, and walked step by step through researching, planning, and implementing a data science workflow on the Titanic dataset — all seamlessly inside VS Code using AI agents.
Try out the HyperVelocity Engineering extension in your own projects, and experiment with the other agents like @RPI-agent to speed up more complex workflows. Happy experimenting!
